sanskrit_devanagari:
स्पर्शान्कृत्वा बहिर्बाह्यांश्चक्षुश्चैवान्तरे भ्रुवो: ।
प्राणापानौ समौ कृत्वा नासाभ्यन्तरचारिणौ ॥ २७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sparśān kṛtvā bahir bāhyāṁś cakṣuś caivāntare bhruvoḥ |
prāṇāpānau samau kṛtvā nāsābhyantara-cāriṇau || 27 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (combined verse 27-28 page on vedabase; this source pack splits the combined Devanagari into the canonical per-verse rendering used by gitasupersite and GRETIL).
exact (separate verse page; transliteration body matches the relevant half of vedabase's combined verse).
exact (academic edition keeps verses separate; body text identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (bgus presents combined commentary on 5.27-28 page; commentary applies to both verses).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_05.027 = MBh_06,027.027).

translations.verbatim_quote: Shutting out all external sense objects, keeping the eyes and vision concentrated between the two eyebrows, suspending the inward and outward breaths within the nostrils, and thus controlling the mind, senses and intelligence, the transcendentalist aiming at liberation becomes free from desire, fear and anger. One who is always in this state is certainly liberated.

Shutting out all external sense objects, keeping the eyes and vision concentrated between the two eyebrows, suspending the inward and outward breaths within the nostrils, and thus controlling the mind, senses and intelligence, the transcendentalist aiming at liberation becomes free from desire, fear and anger. One who is always in this state is certainly liberated.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.27 – 5.28 ‘Shutting off all contact with outside objects,’ i.e., stopping the outward functioning of the senses; seated with his trunk straightened in a posture fit for meditation (Yoga); ‘fixing the gaze between the eye-brows,’ i.e., at the root of the nose where the eye-brows meet; ‘equalising i...

positions.rendering: concentrating the eyes between the two eyebrows (the ājñā-cakra location in some yogic traditions)
fixing the gaze between the eyebrows; literal somatic instruction
the gaze between the brows; somatic preparation for the meditative state

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three traditions read this as literal somatic instruction. The yogic-anatomical mapping (e.g., to the ājñā-cakra in some readings) is preserved across traditions; the engineering analog must be tagged STRETCHED honestly because the verse names a literal physical posture, not a metaphor.

literal_meaning: Shutting out external contacts (sparśān bahir kṛtvā), fixing the gaze between the eyebrows (cakṣur bhruvor antare kṛtvā), and equalising the in-and-out breaths (prāṇa-apānau samau kṛtvā) within the nostrils — [the muni who is destined for liberation, controlling senses-mind-intellect, free of desire-fear-anger, is always liberated. (continued in 5.28)]
traditional_meaning_consensus: Verses 5.27 and 5.28 are read as a single instruction-block by Prabhupada (vedabase combines them onto one page) and as separate but conjoined verses by Shankara and Ramanuja. The instruction is somatic-meditative: external contacts shut, gaze fixed between the eyebrows, breath equalised in the nostrils. This anticipates aṣṭāṅga-yoga's aṅgas (yama, niyama, āsana, prāṇāyāma, pratyāhāra, dhāraṇā, dhyāna, samādhi) which chapter 6 will treat fully. Shankara, Ramanuja, and Prabhupada all read these verses as literal somatic instructions, with the engineering analog stretched honestly past the operational layer.

remaining_gaps: vedabase combines 5.27 and 5.28 onto a single page; this source pack inherits the combined translation/commentary while splitting the Devanagari into per-verse form.
